questions.questionText: A patient develops painful grouped vesicles on an erythematous base in a dermatomal distribution over the right T4 dermatome. Diagnosis?
questions.optionB: Herpes zoster (shingles — VZV reactivation from dorsal root ganglion presenting as painful vesicles in a dermatomal distribution, most common in elderly/immunocompromised)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Dermatomal vesicles = herpes zoster (VZV reactivation). Why correct: VZV (primary = chickenpox) lies dormant in dorsal root ganglia. Reactivation = herpes zoster (shingles): painful grouped vesicles on erythematous base, UNILATERAL, DERMATOMAL. Why A wrong: HSV — recurrent at SAME site (mucocutaneous junction: lips/genitals), non-dermatomal. Why C wrong: Contact dermatitis — itchy, geometric distribution matching contact pattern, not dermatomal. Why D wrong: Impetigo — honey crusts, no dermatomal pattern. Clinical relevance: Complications: post-herpetic neuralgia (most common), herpes zoster ophthalmicus (V1: Hutchinson sign = tip of nose lesion → corneal involvement), Ramsay Hunt syndrome (VII nerve + ear involvement). Treatment: acyclovir/valacyclovir within 72 hours. Exam trap: Multidermatomal/disseminated zoster → suspect immunosuppression (HIV, lymphoma). Memory aid: 'Zoster = Zone (dermatome) + painful Zits (vesicles).' Reference: IADVL Textbook – Viral.
